passage:
Text 1
Nutritionists have observed that people who eat breakfast regularly tend to maintain more stable energy levels throughout the morning compared to those who skip breakfast. One researcher argues that eating a protein-rich breakfast is especially beneficial because protein digests more slowly than carbohydrates, providing a longer-lasting source of energy. The researcher also notes that breakfast skippers often report feeling more fatigued and less focused by mid-morning.

Text 2
A nutrition study tracked 150 adult participants over eight weeks. Each participant was assigned to one of three breakfast groups and completed an energy-level survey at 11 a.m. each day. Average energy ratings (scale of 1–10) were as follows:

| Breakfast Type | Average 11 a.m. Energy Rating |
|---|---|
| No breakfast | 4.1 |
| Carbohydrate-only breakfast | 5.7 |
| Protein-rich breakfast | 7.4 |

passageMetadata.source: Adapted from nutrition research study and dietary recommendations

stem: Which statement is supported by both Text 1 and the data in Text 2?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Eating a protein-rich breakfast is linked to higher mid-morning energy than other options.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Carbohydrate breakfasts provide more energy than protein breakfasts at 11 a.m.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Skipping breakfast results in slightly higher energy than eating carbohydrates only.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Protein digests faster than carbohydrates, causing a quick energy spike.

explanation: Text 1 argues that protein-rich breakfasts are especially beneficial because protein digests slowly, providing longer-lasting energy, and that breakfast skippers feel more fatigued. Text 2 shows that the protein-rich breakfast group had the highest average energy rating at 11 a.m. (7.4), compared to carbohydrate-only (5.7) and no breakfast (4.1). Together, both sources confirm that a protein-rich breakfast is associated with higher mid-morning energy levels.

distractorRationale.B: Text 2 shows protein-rich breakfast (7.4) outperforms carbohydrate-only breakfast (5.7) at 11 a.m., which is the opposite of what this choice claims. This distractor reverses the data's direction.
distractorRationale.C: Text 2 shows that skipping breakfast yields the lowest energy rating (4.1), while eating carbohydrates results in a higher rating (5.7). This distractor misreads the relative values in the table.
distractorRationale.D: Text 1 explicitly states that protein digests more slowly than carbohydrates — not faster. This distractor directly contradicts a stated claim in Text 1 and inverts the mechanism the researcher describes.
